nativeRange.habitat: Trade cultivar selected from Rotala rotundifolia stock for intensified pink-to-magenta coloration; no independent wild range. The parent species occurs as a common weed of rice paddies, marshes, ditches, and the muddy margins of slow-moving streams across temperate and tropical Asia, growing both emersed (rounded leaves) and submersed (narrow lanceolate leaves often flushed wine-red under intense light).

decorPreferences: nutrient-rich aquasoil substrate with iron-rich root tabs
moderate to high light (PAR 50+) needed for the cultivar's signature pink-magenta coloration
CO2 injection recommended for compact form, fast growth, and intense color
soft, slightly acidic water is commonly reported to deepen the pink-magenta hue
regular trimming maintains bushy density and prevents legginess
handle stems gently — leaves are fragile and bruise easily during planting

summary: Rotala Colorata is a cultivar of Rotala rotundifolia selected for intensified pink-to-magenta coloration. It grows readily as a stem plant and is well regarded as a background or midground plant in planted aquariums, producing its deepest reds under moderate-to-high light, CO2 injection, and iron-rich fertilization.
careNotes: Rotala Colorata is a trade cultivar selected from Rotala rotundifolia stock for intensified coloration. Pink and red hues begin to develop under moderate lighting, while deeper magenta tones in the new growth are commonly reported under higher light combined with CO2 injection, iron supplementation, and relatively low nitrate levels. Without adequate light or CO2, the leaves tend to stay olive-green and the cultivar becomes visually indistinguishable from the standard green form of Rotala rotundifolia. Soft, slightly acidic water (pH around 5.5–6.5) is also commonly reported to intensify the pink-magenta expression. Background placement along the rear glass is typical, though the plant can be trimmed regularly into a dense midground bush as well. The leaves are fragile and bruise easily during planting or handling; drastic swings in water parameters can trigger melting, so gradual acclimation is advisable when introducing the plant to a new tank. Related entries: [fw-plant-046] (Rotala rotundifolia parent), [fw-plant-081] (Rotala indica), [fw-plant-162] (Rotala macrandra), [fw-plant-161] (Rotala H'Ra).
breedingNotes: Rotala Colorata is propagated by stem cuttings. Any portion of the stem can be cut and replanted directly into the substrate, where roots and side shoots tend to develop quickly. Pinching off the apical bud (the growing tip at the top of the stem) encourages the mother stem to produce multiple lateral shoots, which can in turn be trimmed and replanted for mass propagation. Cuttings will also root while floating in the water column before planting.

primary.name: Buce Plant — Rotala Colorata
primary.notes: Cultivar-specific retailer care page. Confirms scientific name Rotala rotundifolia 'Colorata', Lythraceae family, moderate-to-high lighting, CO2 recommended, fast growth, background/midground placement, medium-to-difficult care difficulty, stem-cutting propagation, quality aquarium soil substrate, and the color-expression rule that deeper reds require more light + CO2 + nutrients. Also flags fragile leaves and melt risk under drastic parameter changes.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Rotala rotundifolia
additional.notes: Parent-species reference. Confirms binomial Rotala rotundifolia, family Lythraceae, order Myrtales, native range (India, China, Taiwan, Thailand, Laos, Vietnam), paddy/marsh habitat, dimorphic emersed-vs-submersed leaf form (emersed rounded, submerged narrow/lanceolate), and that under intense light 'the leaves can become almost wine red'. Does not address the 'Colorata' cultivar by name.
